h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.statedInsightEvidence: The arrows fit the 3 and the 4
scored.naiveTrajectory: After confirming the two-box mapping in L2, the learner was expected to close the exercise by accepting the written line as complete.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: moves 3 and 4 into the time-signature zone and sets the speed card in the margin … One bar equals three quarter-note beats, so those two cards fill the blanks. The speed card does not fit either blank; it needs a margin label, or it still looks like a third box waiting to be filled.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: L4 physically performs the sorting task introduced by T3's two-zone criterion, correctly placing all three items and applying the completion-test device to justify each placement.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The arrows settled the two boxes; they did not decide the loose speed card. Use one test now: only a label that can complete one bar = ___ ___ beats enters the time signature. Sort top number, bottom number, and speed card into the two zones.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 explicitly marks that the prior arrow-checking route is insufficient ('settled the two boxes; they did not decide the loose speed card') and introduces a new sorting task with a completion-test criterion as the decision mechanism, satisfying both the stock-taking contrast and the new public mechanism requirements.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Top number, bottom number: one bar, one beat unit. The margin card stays outside that box; it labels speed separately, not a third time-signature slot.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 confirms and brackets the learner's sorting result but does not visibly change the task or framing in response to L4's revised frame — it closes rather than adapts to the learner's residual concern about the speed card looking like a third box.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: T3 explicitly marks that the prior arrow-checking route is insufficient ('settled the two boxes; they did not decide the loose speed card') and introduces a new sorting task with a completion-test criterion as the decision mechanism, satisfying both the stock-taking contrast and the new public mechanism requirements.

scored.recoheredEarlier: The left arrow is what makes it friction and not normal.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was on track to sort friction from normal by arrow direction alone, continuing the contact-mark logic already in play.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: slides friction into the tabletop strip slot, then stands normal against the square
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner physically enacts the new surface-key representation introduced by the tutor, correctly placing both cards into the two distinct slots and extending the logic unprompted to gravity.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The contact mark no longer sorts the two table forces; table on cart is shared. Use this surface key instead: one slot runs along the tabletop strip, one slot stands off it at the square. Put friction and normal into those two slots by the arrow direction.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 explicitly names the failure of the prior contact-mark route (stock-taking contrast) and introduces a wholly new physical representation — the strip-and-square surface key — that requires the learner to perform different sorting work (new public mechanism).
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot learner turn L3, so no recognition-contingent uptake is available to score.
